brief_block: In In re Ultra Petroleum Corp., the bankruptcy court established a three-part framework for analyzing whether make-whole amounts constitute unmatured interest: (1) defining interest as "consideration for the use or forbearance of another's money accruing over time," (2) defining unmatured interest as interest that has not accrued as of the petition date, and (3) identifying characteristics that make a debt the economic equivalent of unmatured interest.  In re Ultra Petroleum Corp., 624 B.R. 178 (2020)
opinion_block: To decide whether the Make-Whole Amount is allowed, the Court must define the "economic equivalent of unmatured interest." Pengo, 962 F.2d at 546. The definition is formed in three steps. First, the Court defines interest. Second, the Court defines unmatured interest. Third, the Court identifies the characteristics which make a debt the 'economic equivalent' of unmatured interest.
finding_analysis: The opinion expressly announces a three-step definitional framework that tracks the brief's description almost exactly. The court states that the definition of the economic equivalent of unmatured interest is formed in three steps: defining interest, defining unmatured interest, and identifying the characteristics which make a debt the economic equivalent of unmatured interest. The court then defines interest as consideration for the use or forbearance of another's money accruing over time, and defines unmatured interest as interest that has not accrued or been earned as of the petition date (the order for relief in a bankruptcy case). The brief's paraphrase of the first step uses a verbatim quote from the opinion, and the paraphrases of steps two and three are accurate characterizations of the court's analysis. The quote check flagged similarity at 0.79 because the opinion phrases the first step slightly differently across passages ("Interest means consideration..." vs. "interest is widely understood as consideration..."), but the quoted phrase itself appears verbatim.

proposition: Make-whole amounts are liquidated damages compensating for prepayment losses, not for the use of money, and therefore are not unmatured interest disallowed under Section 502(b)(2).
cited_case: In re Ultra Petroleum Corp., 624 B.R. 178 (2020)
diagnostics: Found in RECAP (not in opinions database). brief_sentence: The court held that make-whole amounts are liquidated damages that compensate for prepayment losses rather than for the use of money, do not accrue over time, and therefore are not unmatured interest subject to disallowance under Section 502(b)(2).  In re Ultra Petroleum Corp., 624 B.R. 178 (2020)
brief_block: The court held that make-whole amounts are liquidated damages that compensate for prepayment losses rather than for the use of money, do not accrue over time, and therefore are not unmatured interest subject to disallowance under Section 502(b)(2).  In re Ultra Petroleum Corp., 624 B.R. 178 (2020)
opinion_block: The Make-Whole Amount represents liquidated damages and should not be characterized as unmatured interest, or its economic equivalent. The Make-Whole Amount is not compensation for the use or forbearance of money, and it does not accrue over time. It is not interest. The Bankruptcy Code allows the Make-Whole Amount.
finding_analysis: The opinion directly supports each element of the brief's paraphrase. The court holds that the Make-Whole Amount represents liquidated damages, that it is not compensation for the use or forbearance of money, that it does not accrue over time, and that it therefore is not interest or unmatured interest and is allowed under the Bankruptcy Code. The brief's characterization tracks the opinion's holding closely, including the two analytical reasons the court gives (not for use of money; does not accrue over time) and the conclusion regarding Section 502(b)(2). No direct quote is asserted, and nothing in the paraphrase overstates the holding.

proposition: Automatic acceleration triggered by a bankruptcy filing changes the maturity date from the original stated date to the petition date, precluding make-whole premium claims.
cited_case: In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
retrieved_case: U.S. Bank Trust National Ass'n v. AMR Corp.
brief_sentence: The Second Circuit established controlling precedent in U.S. Bank Trust National Ass'n v. American Airlines, Inc. (In re AMR Corp.), holding that automatic acceleration triggered by bankruptcy filings changes the maturity date from the original stated date to the petition date, precluding make-whole premium claims.  In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
brief_block: The Second Circuit established controlling precedent in U.S. Bank Trust National Ass'n v. American Airlines, Inc. (In re AMR Corp.), holding that automatic acceleration triggered by bankruptcy filings changes the maturity date from the original stated date to the petition date, precluding make-whole premium claims.  In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
opinion_block: American's bankruptcy petition triggered a default, and this default automatically accelerated the debt. That acceleration 'change[d] the date of maturity from some point in the future . . . to an earlier date based on the debtor's default under the contract.' When the event of default occurred and the debt accelerated, the new maturity date for the debt was November 29, 2011. Consequently, American's attempt to repay the debt in October 2012 was not a voluntary prepayment because '[p]repayment can only occur prior to the maturity date.'
finding_analysis: The brief's characterization of AMR's holding is accurate. The Second Circuit held that the indenture's automatic acceleration provision, triggered by American's voluntary bankruptcy filing, moved the maturity date forward to the petition date (November 29, 2011), so that the later payment was post-maturity rather than a prepayment. Because the indenture conditioned the Make-Whole Amount on voluntary prepayment before maturity, no premium was owed. The brief captures both the mechanism (automatic acceleration shifts maturity) and the consequence (no make-whole) that the court adopted.

brief_block: The Second Circuit reinforced this principle in Matter of MPM Silicones, L.L.C., rejecting noteholders' arguments that issuing replacement notes under a Chapter 11 plan constituted an "optional redemption" triggering make-whole premiums.  Matter of MPM Silicones, L.L.C., 874 F.3d 787 (2017)

The court expressly rejected the Senior-Lien Notes holders' argument that issuing replacement notes under the Chapter 11 plan was an optional redemption triggering the make-whole premium, both because post-acceleration payments are post-maturity (per AMR) and because the replacement notes were issued automatically by operation of the Acceleration Clauses rather than at MPM's option. proposition: Under New York law, a lender generally forfeits the right to a prepayment premium by accelerating the loan following default because acceleration advances the maturity date and eliminates the possibility of prepayment.
cited_case: In re 1141 Realty Owner LLC, 598 B.R. 534 (2019)
brief_sentence: New York law generally provides that lenders forfeit the right to prepayment premiums by accelerating loans following default because acceleration advances the maturity date, eliminating the possibility of prepayment. In re 1141 Realty Owner LLC, 598 B.R. 534 (2019)
brief_block: New York law generally provides that lenders forfeit the right to prepayment premiums by accelerating loans following default because acceleration advances the maturity date, eliminating the possibility of prepayment. In re 1141 Realty Owner LLC, 598 B.R. 534 (2019)
opinion_block: Generally, a lender that accelerates a loan following a default forfeits the right to a prepayment premium because the acceleration advances the maturity date, and by definition, the loan cannot be prepaid.
finding_analysis: The opinion articulates the general forfeiture rule in substantively identical terms: acceleration advances the maturity date and eliminates the possibility of prepayment. The brief's paraphrase accurately captures the rule and the reasoning.

proposition: Two exceptions to the forfeiture rule exist: intentional default to evade the premium, and contracts with clear and unambiguous language requiring premium payment even after default and acceleration.
cited_case: In re 1141 Realty Owner LLC, 598 B.R. 534 (2019)
brief_sentence: However, courts recognize two exceptions: (1) when borrowers intentionally default to trigger acceleration and evade premium payment, and (2) when contracts contain "clear and unambiguous" clauses requiring premium payment even after default and acceleration. In re 1141 Realty Owner LLC, 598 B.R. 534 (2019)

opinion_block: Courts recognize two exceptions to this general rule. First, if a clear and unambiguous clause requires the payment of the prepayment premium even after default and acceleration, the clause will be analyzed as a liquidated damages clause. . . . Second, if the borrower intentionally defaults to trigger the acceleration and "evade" payment of the prepayment premium, the lender can enforce the prepayment premium.
finding_analysis: The opinion enumerates the same two exceptions, and the phrase "clear and unambiguous" appears verbatim. The brief reverses the opinion's ordering (intentional default is listed second in the opinion, first in the brief), but the substance of both exceptions is faithfully reproduced.

opinion_block: Section 2.3.3 mandates the payment of the Yield Maintenance Default Premium as a matter of New York contract law. It imposes the make-whole premium in connection with any payment made after an Event of Default, not just a prepayment made after an Event of Default but before acceleration. In fact, the word acceleration does not appear in the clause. Instead, the clause deems the post-default payment, whenever made, to be a "voluntary prepayment" for the purpose of the Yield Maintenance Default Premium.
finding_analysis: The opinion squarely holds that the Section 2.3.3 provision deeming any post-default payment a "voluntary prepayment" enforced the yield maintenance premium notwithstanding acceleration, rendering acceleration irrelevant. The brief's description of the court's reasoning and outcome is accurate.

proposition: In AMR, the Second Circuit rejected the argument that refinancing to obtain better rates constituted voluntary redemption when the indenture's automatic acceleration provisions had already operated.
cited_case: In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
retrieved_case: U.S. Bank Trust National Ass'n v. AMR Corp.
brief_sentence: In AMR, the Second Circuit rejected arguments that refinancing to access better rates constituted voluntary redemption when the indenture's acceleration provisions had already operated automatically.  In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
brief_block: In AMR, the Second Circuit rejected arguments that refinancing to access better rates constituted voluntary redemption when the indenture's acceleration provisions had already operated automatically.  In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
opinion_block: U.S. Bank argues, finally, that regardless whether American's November 2011 bankruptcy petition was an Event of Default triggering debt acceleration, American's effort in October 2012 to pay off its debt constitutes a Section 2.11 voluntary redemption for which Section 3.02 provides for payment of a Make-Whole Amount. The bankruptcy court rejected this argument, holding that the Debtors' proposed payment is a 'post-maturity date repayment, not a prepayment' . . . . We agree. . . . U.S. Bank argues, next, that Section 3.03, by its terms, only excludes payment of a Make-Whole Amount 'as a consequence of or in connection with an Event of Default or the acceleration of the Equipment Notes,' and that the post-maturity repayment that American now attempts is not, in fact, 'a consequence of or in connection with' either its bankruptcy filing or debt acceleration but is, instead, an attempt to take advantage of low interest rates. We need not parse the meanings of 'consequence' or 'connection' to reject U.S. Bank's interpretation.
finding_analysis: The brief's description matches the opinion. American moved in October 2012 to refinance its debt 'citing low interest rates available in the market,' and U.S. Bank argued that such opportunistic refinancing should be treated as a voluntary redemption owing a Make-Whole Amount. The Second Circuit rejected that argument, holding the payment was a post-maturity repayment governed by Section 3.03 rather than a Section 2.11 voluntary redemption, because automatic acceleration under Section 4.02(a)(i) had already advanced the maturity date to the petition date. The brief's phrase 'the indenture's acceleration provisions had already operated automatically' tracks the court's reasoning about Section 4.02(a)(i)'s self-executing effect on the Section 4.01(g) bankruptcy default.

proposition: The solvent-versus-insolvent debtor distinction may influence a court's willingness to enforce carefully drafted make-whole provisions when enforcement does not disadvantage other creditors.
cited_case: In re Chemtura Corp., 439 B.R. 561 (2010)
brief_sentence: The distinction between solvent and insolvent debtors may influence courts' willingness to enforce carefully drafted provisions, with the Chemtura decision suggesting greater deference to contractual rights when enforcement does not disadvantage other creditors.  In re Chemtura Corp., 439 B.R. 561 (2010)
brief_block: The distinction between solvent and insolvent debtors may influence courts' willingness to enforce carefully drafted provisions, with the Chemtura decision suggesting greater deference to contractual rights when enforcement does not disadvantage other creditors.  In re Chemtura Corp., 439 B.R. 561 (2010)
opinion_block: With a solvent debtor, issues as to fairness amongst creditors, in sharing a limited pie, no longer apply; the allowance of claims under a make-whole provision, or for damages for breach of a no-call, no longer comes at the expense of other creditors. Thus it's at least arguable, as stated in Charles & Kleinhaus, that "[t]he effect of prepayment clauses in solvent cases, therefore, should be an issue of state law alone." . . . As matters of bankruptcy law, since we here have a solvent debtor, I think the bondholders are likely to get whatever they're entitled to under state law, on both issues of notes.
finding_analysis: Although no verbatim quotations are offered, the brief's paraphrase accurately tracks the opinion's reasoning. The court explicitly draws the solvent/insolvent distinction, explains that fairness-among-creditors concerns fall away in the solvent context because enforcement does not come at other creditors' expense, and concludes that in a solvent case the bondholders are likely to get whatever state law gives them. That is precisely the "greater deference to contractual rights when enforcement does not disadvantage other creditors" point the brief attributes to Chemtura.
